stem: A four-month-old has been on humidified high-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min with FiO2 0.40 for four hours for RSV bronchiolitis. Mother says he breastfed only briefly before tiring. On examination there is marked substernal recession, head bob, and audible grunting. Capillary blood gas: pH 7.28, pCO2 58 mmHg, HCO3 22 mmol/L, lactate 1.4 mmol/L. Chest X-ray shows hyperinflation with no focal consolidation. The paediatric retrieval team has been contacted.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 178, BP 78/45 mmHg, RR 68, SpO2 89%, temperature 37.9°C
data_table.Examination: Substernal recession, head bob, grunting, lethargic but rousable
data_table.Investigations: Capillary blood gas pH 7.28, pCO2 58 mmHg, HCO3 22 mmol/L, lactate 1.4 mmol/L; chest X-ray hyperinflated, no focal consolidation
data_table.Medications: Humidified HFNC 2 L/kg/min, FiO2 0.40 for 4 hours

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Initiate CPAP at 6 cm H2O via retrieval team prior to PICU transfer
options.rationale: Rising pCO2 with persistent severe work of breathing on the maximal RCH HFNC ceiling defines HFNC failure. CPAP is the next non-invasive escalation, started during retrieval and continued in PICU. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Endotracheal intubation and conventional ventilation immediately
options.rationale: Intubation is reserved for apnoea, exhaustion, or CPAP failure. With lactate 1.4 mmol/L and no shock, a CPAP trial precedes intubation. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Increase HFNC flow to 3 L/kg/min and reassess after one hour
options.rationale: RCH ceiling for HFNC in bronchiolitis is 2 L/kg/min. Further flow does not improve outcomes and delays appropriate escalation. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg with hypertonic saline 3% nebuliser
options.rationale: Salbutamol and hypertonic saline are not recommended for routine bronchiolitis under 12 months in Australia and do not reverse respiratory failure. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg empirically for bacterial co-infection
options.rationale: Bacterial co-infection is uncommon in bronchiolitis. With a clear viral cause and no focal X-ray finding, empirical antibiotics are not indicated. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: This infant meets HFNC-failure criteria: persistent severe work of breathing with rising pCO2 (58 mmHg) and SpO2 below the RCH target of 90% on maximal flow. The correct response is non-invasive escalation to CPAP, ideally during PICU retrieval. Intubation is held back for apnoea, GCS drop, or CPAP failure. Increasing HFNC above 2 L/kg/min has no evidence base and delays appropriate escalation.
key_points: RCH bronchiolitis HFNC ceiling is 2 L/kg/min
HFNC failure criteria: persistent severe WOB, rising pCO2, SpO2 below 90% target
CPAP precedes intubation when no apnoea or shock
explanation.pearls: If pCO2 rises above 55 mmHg on maximal HFNC, escalate non-invasively within 30 minutes; intubate for apnoea or GCS drop, not for the gas number alone.
